name: Bytecode Quickening and Specialising Interpreters
summary: Brunthaler ECOOP 2010: replace generic bytecodes with type-specialised variants at runtime. CPython 3.11 PEP 659 brought this to mainline Python — `LOAD_ATTR` rewrites in place to `LOAD_ATTR_INSTANCE_VALUE` based on observed object shapes. 10–60% runtime improvement without a JIT.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Stefan Brunthaler's **bytecode quickening** technique (ECOOP 2010) replaces generic bytecodes with type-specialised variants at runtime. The first execution of `BINARY_ADD` observes its operand types; subsequent executions are rewritten in place to a specialised opcode like `BINARY_ADD_INT` that skips the type-dispatch prologue. This amounts to inline caching but inside the interpreter dispatch loop instead of JIT-compiled code.

sections.title: CPython PEP 659
sections.content:
**CPython 3.11's specialising adaptive interpreter** (PEP 659, Mark Shannon 2021) brought this technique to mainline Python. The interpreter observes call sites and rewrites `LOAD_ATTR` into forms like `LOAD_ATTR_INSTANCE_VALUE` (fast path for common layouts) or `LOAD_ATTR_SLOT` based on observed object shapes. A counter decays on miss, and families de-optimise back to the generic form if specialisation fails.

Python 3.11 gained 10–60% runtime improvement primarily from this mechanism — without a JIT. The improvement is per-opcode, multiplied across the millions of hot opcodes a typical Python program executes, so the cumulative speedup compounds.

sections.title: Design lesson
sections.content:
The insight: you don't need to generate machine code to get inline-caching benefits. Interpreter-level specialisation captures the same monomorphic-fast-path wins with a fraction of the engineering complexity. The trade-off is favourable on both axes — large baseline speedup, low architectural cost — which makes it a strong candidate to evaluate before committing to a JIT.

For a new language designer, the lesson is that bytecode quickening is one of the highest-leverage interpreter optimisations available, and substantially cheaper to implement than a baseline JIT. CPython proved it works on a real-world dynamic-language workload at production scale.
